result.markdown:
--- title: "Moderator Analysis: Explaining the Heterogeneity" subtitle: "Meta-Analytic Update of Mate-Choice Copying (MCC)" author: "Eduardo S. A. Santos & Collaborators" date: today format: html: toc: true toc-depth: 3 theme: cosmo --- ## Why a Moderator Analysis? {#sec-motivation} The overall synthesis ([Heterogeneity Decomposition](overall_effect.qmd#sec-heterogeneity)) showed that the updated evidence base is dominated by **persistent, largely unexplained heterogeneity**. Total $I^2$ was high across every model, yet very little of that variance sat at the *study* or *phylogenetic* level. Instead it accumulated at the **within-study (observation)** and **non-phylogenetic species** levels — exactly where experimental and ecological distinctions among individual trials live. The disagreement among mate-choice copying estimates is therefore not mainly a matter of some laboratories producing systematically larger effects, nor of copying being concentrated in particular lineages; it points to **genuine biological and methodological moderators** that the intercept-only models could not capture. During extraction we collected several candidate moderators that are plausible sources of this within-study/within-species variation: * **Copying mechanism** — *generalized* (copying a *type* of male) versus *individual* (copying a *specific* demonstrated male); * **Modality of the social cue** — the sensory channel of the demonstration (visual, chemical, or acoustic); * **Virginity of the observer** — whether the choosing individual was sexually naïve; * **Relative age of the demonstrator** — the age of the demonstrator relative to the observer. This chapter does two things, in order: 1. **Diagnostics (Part 1).** Before fitting anything, we audit each candidate moderator *in the new extraction* — how its levels are populated, how much is missing, and how many studies/species back each level — and we check whether the coding can be reconciled with the original meta-analyses. This determines **which** moderators are analysable and **on which dataset** (new-only versus combined). 2. **Uni-moderator models (Part 2).** For the moderators that pass the diagnostic, we fit single-moderator multilevel models using the same `rma.mv` machinery, omnibus $Q_M$ test, and partial-$I^2$ heterogeneity decomposition used elsewhere in this workflow. We report two things for each: (i) whether the moderator explains a meaningful share of the *heterogeneity* (the $Q_M$ / $I^2$ question), and (ii) the **direction and magnitude of the differences between levels** ([§Biologically Meaningful Level Differences](#sec-biological)) — because even when a contrast is not statistically distinct, its sign can speak to specific hypotheses raised in the main text (most notably whether virgin observers copy more, as the original synthesis reported). > These analyses are **exploratory**. With the dataset at its current size, several moderator levels are thin, and we have not pre-registered these tests. We report them to map the most promising directions for future, ideally pre-registered, syntheses — not as confirmatory claims. --- ## Data Loading & Moderator Construction {#sec-data} We load the new extraction and derive the four candidate moderators from the extraction columns. We *also* load the moderator columns from the two original datasets (Davies et al. 2020 for Hedges' $g$; Jones & DuVal 2019 for the log odds ratio) so we can later test whether any moderator can be harmonised into a **combined** analysis. Each effect size in the new extraction carries **both** a Hedges' $d$ and an odds ratio, so the new-extraction moderator coverage is identical across the two effect-size scales. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} path_new <- here::here("data", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline", "Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx") path_davies <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Davies_et_al_2020_Final_data.xlsx") path_jones <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Jones_DuVal_2019_data.CSV") db_new_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_new, sheet = "EduardoExtraction") db_davies <- readxl::read_excel(path_davies, sheet = "Data") db_jones <- readr::read_csv(path_jones, show_col_types = FALSE) # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # New extraction: filter excluded rows, coerce effect sizes, derive moderators. # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Modality recode: the extraction column `experimentalTypeStimulus` records the # *type of demonstration*, which maps onto a sensory channel as follows — # Copulation / Association / Presence of eggs in nest -> Visual # Odour stimuli -> Chemical # Acoustic stimuli -> Acoustic # `demo_type` keeps the two well-populated *visual* sub-types (Copulation vs # Association) for a focused, better-powered sensitivity contrast. db_new <- db_new_raw %>% filter(is.na(RemoveFromDataset) | RemoveFromDataset != "Yes") %>% mutate( across(c( effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD, effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance, effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio, effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance ), as.numeric), logOddsRatio = log(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio), logOddsRatioVariance = effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance, stim_raw = str_squish(experimentalTypeStimulus), mech = factor(str_squish(experimentalTypeCopyingMechanism), levels = c("Generalized", "Individual")), modality = factor(dplyr::case_when( stim_raw %in% c("Copulation", "Association", "Presence of eggs in nest") ~ "Visual", stim_raw %in% c("Odour stimuli") ~ "Chemical", stim_raw %in% c("Acoustic stimuli") ~ "Acoustic", TRUE ~ NA_character_ ), levels = c("Visual", "Chemical", "Acoustic")), demo_type = factor(dplyr::case_when( stim_raw == "Copulation" ~ "Copulation", stim_raw == "Association" ~ "Association", TRUE ~ NA_character_ ), levels = c("Association", "Copulation")), virgin = factor(str_squish(experimentalTypeVirgin), levels = c("No", "Yes")), demo_age = factor(dplyr::na_if(str_squish(experimentalModelAge), "NA")) ) # Tidy per-scale new-extraction frames (yi, vi, IDs, species epithet, moderators) make_new <- function(yi_col, vi_col) { db_new %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi_col]]), !is.na(.data[[vi_col]]), .data[[vi_col]] > 0) %>% transmute( yi = .data[[yi_col]], vi = .data[[vi_col]], identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)), mech, modality, demo_type, virgin, demo_age, Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)" ) } new_hedges <- make_new("effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD", "effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance") new_or <- make_new("logOddsRatio", "logOddsRatioVariance") # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- # Original datasets: extract ONLY the moderator columns we may be able to # harmonise, with their native codings, for the diagnostic + combined models. # * Davies (Hedges' g): `Generalised_or_individual` -> copying mechanism # * Jones (log OR) : `Virgin` (Y/N) -> observer virginity # --------------------------------------------------------------------------- davies_hedges <- db_davies %>% mutate(yi = as.numeric(Hedges_d_directional), vi = as.numeric(Variance)) %>% filter(!is.na(yi), !is.na(vi), vi > 0) %>% transmute( yi, vi, identifierStudyId = as.character(Author), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(Effect_size_no), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(Species_latin), -1)), mech = dplyr::recode(str_squish(Generalised_or_individual), "Generalised" = "Generalized"), Dataset_Source = "Original (Davies 2020)" ) %>% mutate(mech = factor(mech, levels = c("Generalized", "Individual"))) jones_or <- db_jones %>% filter(!is.na(lnOR), !is.na(VlnOR), VlnOR > 0) %>% transmute( yi = lnOR, vi = VlnOR, identifierStudyId = as.character(study), identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(study_Exp), taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(as.character(Species)), virgin = dplyr::recode(str_squish(Virgin), "Y" = "Yes", "N" = "No"), Dataset_Source = "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" ) %>% mutate(virgin = factor(virgin, levels = c("No", "Yes"))) # Combined frames for the two harmonisable moderators comb_hedges_mech <- bind_rows( new_hedges %>% select(yi, vi, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies, mech, Dataset_Source), davies_hedges %>% select(yi, vi, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies, mech, Dataset_Source) ) comb_or_virgin <- bind_rows( new_or %>% select(yi, vi, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies, virgin, Dataset_Source), jones_or %>% select(yi, vi, identifierStudyId, identifierEffectSizeID, taxonomySpecies, virgin, Dataset_Source) ) ``` ::: --- ## Part 1 — Moderator Diagnostics {#sec-diagnostics} ### Candidate moderators and their extraction columns ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Coverage in the new extraction {#sec-coverage} For each candidate moderator we tabulate, per level, the number of effect sizes (`k`), the number of distinct studies and species backing that level, and the percentage of effect sizes with a missing value. A moderator is only worth modelling if it **varies**, if **each level is backed by more than one study** (so the moderator is not perfectly confounded with a single study), and if **missingness is low**. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### The social-cue modality recode {#sec-modality-recode} The extraction column `experimentalTypeStimulus` records the *form of the demonstration* rather than a sensory label directly, so we map it onto a modality: | `experimentalTypeStimulus` | Sensory modality | |---|---| | Copulation, Association, Presence of eggs in nest | **Visual** | | Odour stimuli | **Chemical** | | Acoustic stimuli | **Acoustic** | The coverage table makes the central limitation plain: the new extraction is **overwhelmingly visual**, with the *Chemical* and *Acoustic* levels each resting on a handful of effect sizes from very few studies. A three-level modality model can be fitted, but its non-visual contrasts are effectively confounded with one or two studies/species and must be read with extreme caution. We therefore also define `demo_type` — *Copulation* versus *Association*, the two well-populated visual sub-types — as a better-powered sensitivity contrast within the dominant modality. ### Harmonisation with the original meta-analyses {#sec-harmonisation} The four moderators were coded under a single protocol in the **new extraction**, but the original datasets recorded only some of them, and not always on a compatible scale. The table below records, for each moderator, whether a compatible variable exists in Davies et al. 2020 (Hedges' $g$) and Jones & DuVal 2019 (log OR), and the resulting verdict. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Diagnostic verdict {#sec-verdict} Putting the coverage and harmonisation evidence together: * **Copying mechanism (Generalized vs Individual)** — well balanced in the new extraction (37 vs 32 effect sizes, each from many studies) and coded identically by Davies. **Analysable new-only on both scales, and combinable on the Hedges' $g$ scale.** * **Observer virginity (No vs Yes)** — both levels well populated (21 vs 48) and coded identically by Jones & DuVal. **Analysable new-only on both scales, and combinable on the log OR scale.** * **Social-cue modality (Visual/Chemical/Acoustic)** — analysable **new-only**, but the non-visual levels are sparse and study-confounded; treated as exploratory, with the Copulation-vs-Association visual contrast as a sensitivity check. The original "demonstration type" codes a different construct and cannot be merged. * **Demonstrator relative age** — **dropped**: the new extraction records 1 non-missing level(s) ("Same-age"), i.e. essentially **no variation**, so it cannot enter a model. The original datasets carry no compatible variable. **Bottom line.** Three moderators (mechanism, modality, virginity) can be tested on the **new dataset** for both effect-size scales. Two of them can additionally be tested on a **combined dataset** where the construct harmonises cleanly — mechanism on Hedges' $g$ (new + Davies) and virginity on log OR (new + Jones). Demonstrator age is not analysable. Part 2 implements exactly these models. --- ## Part 2 — Uni-Moderator Models {#sec-models} ### Modelling approach Each model is a single-moderator multilevel meta-analysis fitted with `metafor::rma.mv()`, mirroring the [taxonomic-moderator model](overall_effect.qmd#sec-taxonomic-moderator): * **Dependency.** A variance–covariance matrix from `vcalc()` ($\rho = 0.5$) handles multiple effect sizes within a study. * **Random effects.** Study (`identifierStudyId`), residual effect-size (`identifierEffectSizeID`), and a non-phylogenetic species intercept (`taxonomySpecies`). The phylogenetic term is **omitted**: the heterogeneity decomposition showed it carries negligible variance, and dropping it keeps these smaller moderator models identifiable (the same choice made for the taxonomic model). * **Heteroscedasticity with a fallback cascade.** We first attempt a heteroscedastic structure that lets the **residual (within-study) variance differ by moderator level** (`~ moderator | effect-size`, `struct = "HCS"`, `rho = 0`), trying several optimisers; if it errors we fall back to a homoscedastic structure. This is the `orchaRd` 2.0 recommendation for meta-regression with heterogeneous variances [@nakagawaOrchaRd2Visualising2023]. * **Inference.** The moderator is intercept-coded (`mods = ~ moderator`); per-level means, CIs, and prediction intervals come from `orchaRd::mod_results()` (marginalised means via `emmeans`), and the among-level test is the model's omnibus $Q_M$. Heterogeneity is decomposed with partial $I^2$ from `orchaRd::i2_ml()`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Generalised heteroscedastic single-moderator fitter (adapted from # fit_taxon_hetero_mv in overall_effect.qmd). Returns the chosen model plus a # cascade log so the reader can see which structure was actually used. fit_mod_hetero <- function(dat, group_var, group_levels, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) { dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0, !is.na(.data[[group_var]])) if (nrow(dat2) == 0) return(NULL) dat2[[group_var]] <- droplevels(factor(dat2[[group_var]], levels = group_levels)) VCV <- metafor::vcalc(vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2) mods_f <- stats::as.formula(paste0("~ ", group_var)) obs_f <- stats::as.formula(paste0("~ ", group_var, " | ", obs_var)) opt_grid <- list( "nlminb" = list(optimizer = "nlminb", iter.max = 10000, eval.max = 10000), "optim/BFGS" = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "BFGS", maxit = 10000), "optim/L-BFGS-B" = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "L-BFGS-B", maxit = 10000), "optim/Nelder-Mead" = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "Nelder-Mead", maxit = 50000, reltol = 1e-9) ) build <- function(cand, ctrl) { args <- c( list(yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, mods = mods_f, random = cand$random, test = "t", dfs = "contain", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2), cand$args ) if (!is.null(cand$struct)) args$struct <- cand$struct if (!is.null(ctrl)) args$control <- ctrl do.call(metafor::rma.mv, args) } candidates <- list( list(spec = "Heteroscedastic (residual only, per level; HCS, rho = 0)", random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, obs_f, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), struct = "HCS", args = list(rho = 0), het = TRUE), list(spec = "Homoscedastic (single variance per component)", random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), struct = NULL, args = list(), het = FALSE) ) fit_once <- function(cand, ctrl) { warns <- character(0); t0 <- Sys.time() mod <- tryCatch( withCallingHandlers(build(cand, ctrl), warning = function(w) { warns[[length(warns) + 1L]] <<- conditionMessage(w); invokeRestart("muffleWarning") }), error = function(e) structure(list(msg = conditionMessage(e)), class = "mod_fit_error") ) list(mod = mod, warns = warns, secs = as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))) } attempt <- function(cand) { if (!isTRUE(cand$het)) { r <- fit_once(cand, NULL); failed <- inherits(r$mod, "mod_fit_error") return(list(model = if (failed) NULL else r$mod, failed = failed, error = if (failed) r$mod$msg else NA_character_, warnings = r$warns, secs = r$secs, optimizer = "default (nlminb)")) } msgs <- character(0); total <- 0 for (onm in names(opt_grid)) { r <- fit_once(cand, opt_grid[[onm]]); total <- total + r$secs if (!inherits(r$mod, "mod_fit_error")) { return(list(model = r$mod, failed = FALSE, error = NA_character_, warnings = r$warns, secs = total, optimizer = onm)) } msgs <- c(msgs, paste0(onm, ": ", r$mod$msg)) } list(model = NULL, failed = TRUE, error = paste(msgs, collapse = " || "), warnings = character(0), secs = total, optimizer = NA_character_) } diag_rows <- list(); chosen <- NULL; chosen_spec <- NA_character_ for (i in seq_along(candidates)) { cand <- candidates[[i]] if (!is.null(chosen)) { diag_rows[[i]] <- tibble::tibble(stage = i, structure = cand$spec, status = "not attempted", optimizer = NA_character_, seconds = NA_real_, n_warnings = NA_integer_, detail = "an earlier structure succeeded") next } a <- attempt(cand) status <- if (a$failed) "ERROR" else if (length(a$warnings) > 0) "OK (with warnings)" else "OK" detail <- if (a$failed) a$error else if (length(a$warnings) > 0) paste(a$warnings, collapse = " || ") else "clean fit" diag_rows[[i]] <- tibble::tibble(stage = i, structure = cand$spec, status = status, optimizer = a$optimizer, seconds = round(a$secs, 2), n_warnings = length(a$warnings), detail = detail) if (!a$failed) { chosen <- a$model; chosen_spec <- cand$spec } } diagnostics <- dplyr::bind_rows(diag_rows) if (is.null(chosen)) { warning("All structures failed for moderator '", group_var, "'.") return(list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = NULL, spec = "ALL FAILED", diagnostics = diagnostics, group_var = group_var)) } list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = chosen, spec = chosen_spec, diagnostics = diagnostics, group_var = group_var) } # Omnibus among-level test (QM tests all non-intercept coefficients jointly). among_test <- function(res) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) return(NULL) list(QM = res$model$QM, QMp = res$model$QMp, QMdf = res$model$QMdf) } ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Moderator registry: each entry is fitted on both new-extraction scales. mod_specs <- list( mech = list(var = "mech", levels = c("Generalized", "Individual"), label = "Copying mechanism", palette = c("Generalized" = "#E69F00", "Individual" = "#56B4E9")), modality = list(var = "modality", levels = c("Visual", "Chemical", "Acoustic"), label = "Social-cue modality", palette = c("Visual" = "#009E73", "Chemical" = "#CC79A7", "Acoustic" = "#D55E00")), demo_type = list(var = "demo_type", levels = c("Association", "Copulation"), label = "Demonstration sub-type (visual)", palette = c("Association" = "#0072B2", "Copulation" = "#E69F00")), virgin = list(var = "virgin", levels = c("No", "Yes"), label = "Observer virginity", palette = c("No" = "#0072B2", "Yes" = "#D55E00")) ) # Fit every moderator on the new-extraction Hedges' g and log OR frames. fits_new <- list() for (key in names(mod_specs)) { s <- mod_specs[[key]] fits_new[[key]] <- list( hedges = fit_mod_hetero(new_hedges, s$var, s$levels), or = fit_mod_hetero(new_or, s$var, s$levels) ) } # Combined-dataset extensions (harmonisable moderators only). fit_comb_mech_hedges <- fit_mod_hetero(comb_hedges_mech, "mech", c("Generalized", "Individual")) fit_comb_virgin_or <- fit_mod_hetero(comb_or_virgin, "virgin", c("No", "Yes")) # Persist all fits so they can be inspected without re-rendering. tryCatch({ d <- here::here("outputs", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline") dir.create(d, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE) saveRDS(list(new = fits_new, comb_mech_hedges = fit_comb_mech_hedges, comb_virgin_or = fit_comb_virgin_or, saved_at = Sys.time()), file.path(d, "moderator_model_fits.rds")) }, error = function(e) warning("Could not save moderator_model_fits.rds: ", conditionMessage(e))) ``` ::: ### Reporting helpers ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Partial I^2 (long form) for a fitted moderator model. comp_labels <- c( Total = "Total", identifierStudyId = "Study", identifierEffectSizeID = "Effect size (residual)", taxonomySpecies = "Species (non-phylogenetic)" ) # orchaRd::i2_ml() does not support the heteroscedastic (HCS) structure that the # cascade prefers, so for the partial-I² *decomposition* we compute i2_ml on a # homoscedastic refit of the same data (one variance per random effect). This is # exactly how the overall synthesis reports partial I² (from the homoscedastic # intercept models); the heteroscedastic model is retained for the means/Q_M. i2_homosc_refit <- function(res) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model) || is.null(res$data)) return(NULL) d <- res$data m <- tryCatch( metafor::rma.mv( yi = d$yi, V = res$VCV, mods = stats::as.formula(paste0("~ ", res$group_var)), random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies), test = "t", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = d ), error = function(e) NULL ) if (is.null(m)) return(NULL) tryCatch(orchaRd::i2_ml(m), error = function(e) NULL) } get_i2_long <- function(res, analysis) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) return(NULL) v <- tryCatch(orchaRd::i2_ml(res$model), error = function(e) NULL) if (is.null(v)) v <- i2_homosc_refit(res) if (is.null(v)) return(NULL) tibble::tibble(Analysis = analysis, Component = sub("^I2_", "", names(v)), I2 = round(as.numeric(v), 1)) %>% mutate(Component = dplyr::recode(Component, !!!comp_labels)) } # Total I^2: orchaRd on the fitted model, else on the homoscedastic refit (i2_ml # does not support the HCS structure), else a Higgins typical-variance fallback. total_i2 <- function(res) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) return(NA_real_) i2 <- tryCatch(orchaRd::i2_ml(res$model), error = function(e) NULL) if (is.null(i2)) i2 <- i2_homosc_refit(res) if (!is.null(i2) && any(grepl("Total", names(i2)))) return(as.numeric(i2[grep("Total", names(i2))[1]])) m <- res$model; vi <- res$data$vi; wi <- 1 / vi; k <- length(vi) s2 <- (k - 1) * sum(wi) / (sum(wi)^2 - sum(wi^2)) vc <- sum(c(m$sigma2, m$tau2, m$gamma2), na.rm = TRUE) 100 * vc / (vc + s2) } # Per-level marginalised means table. level_means <- function(res, analysis) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) return(NULL) mr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res$model, mod = res$group_var, group = "identifierStudyId") mr$mod_table %>% transmute(Analysis = analysis, Level = name, Estimate = round(estimate, 3), `CI lower` = round(lowerCL, 3), `CI upper` = round(upperCL, 3), `PI lower` = round(lowerPR, 3), `PI upper` = round(upperPR, 3)) } # Between-level contrasts: each non-intercept model coefficient is the # difference between that level and the reference level, on the effect-size # scale. A statistically "distinct" difference would have a CI excluding zero; # a biologically suggestive one is read from the sign and magnitude even when the # CI overlaps zero. For log OR models we also report the multiplicative odds # ratio (exp(difference)) so the contrast is interpretable on the OR scale. level_contrasts <- function(res, analysis, is_or = FALSE) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) return(NULL) m <- res$model nm <- rownames(m$b) keep <- !grepl("intrcpt|intercept", nm, ignore.case = TRUE) if (!any(keep)) return(NULL) ref <- levels(droplevels(factor(res$data[[res$group_var]], levels = levels(res$data[[res$group_var]]))))[1] out <- tibble::tibble( Analysis = analysis, Contrast = paste0(sub(res$group_var, "", nm[keep]), " − ", ref), Difference = round(as.numeric(m$b)[keep], 3), `CI lower` = round(m$ci.lb[keep], 3), `CI upper` = round(m$ci.ub[keep], 3), p = round(m$pval[keep], 3) ) if (is_or) { out$`Odds ratio` <- round(exp(out$Difference), 2) out <- dplyr::relocate(out, `Odds ratio`, .after = Difference) } out } # Orchard plot for a fitted moderator model. plot_mod_orchard <- function(res, palette, xlab, title) { if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) return(NULL) mr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res$model, mod = res$group_var, group = "identifierStudyId") oc <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(mr, xlab = xlab, angle = 0, g = TRUE, k = TRUE) oc <- tryCatch(add_ci_labels(oc, mr), error = function(e) { warning("add_ci_labels failed: ", conditionMessage(e)); oc }) oc + ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = palette, name = NULL, aesthetics = c("colour", "fill")) + ggplot2::ggtitle(title) + ggplot2::theme(plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5), legend.position = "none") } # Compact omnibus + I2 one-liner for a model. qm_line <- function(res, scale_label) { a <- among_test(res); i2 <- total_i2(res) if (is.null(a)) return(paste0("*", scale_label, ":* model did not fit.")) paste0("*", scale_label, ":* total $I^2$ = ", fmt(i2, 1), "%; omnibus $Q_M$ = ", fmt(a$QM, 2), " (", pstr(a$QMp), "), a **", sigw(a$QMp), "** difference among levels.") } ``` ::: ### New-extraction models {#sec-new-models} Each tab below presents one moderator, with the Hedges' $g$ and log odds ratio models side by side: the omnibus test and total heterogeneity, the per-level means, the partial-$I^2$ decomposition, the fit-cascade log, and the orchard plots. ::: {.panel-tabset} #### Copying mechanism *Hedges' g:* total $I^2$ = 81.0%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 0.31 (p = 0.582), a **non-significant** difference among levels. *Log odds ratio:* total $I^2$ = 81.0%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 0.30 (p = 0.587), a **non-significant** difference among levels.
![](moderator_analysis_files/figure-html/new_model_panels-1.png){width=960} #### Social-cue modality *Hedges' g:* total $I^2$ = 81.7%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 0.18 (p = 0.839), a **non-significant** difference among levels. *Log odds ratio:* total $I^2$ = 81.7%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 0.18 (p = 0.836), a **non-significant** difference among levels.
![](moderator_analysis_files/figure-html/new_model_panels-2.png){width=960} #### Demonstration sub-type (visual) *Hedges' g:* total $I^2$ = 84.6%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 1.13 (p = 0.293), a **non-significant** difference among levels. *Log odds ratio:* total $I^2$ = 84.5%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 1.13 (p = 0.293), a **non-significant** difference among levels.
![](moderator_analysis_files/figure-html/new_model_panels-3.png){width=960} #### Observer virginity *Hedges' g:* total $I^2$ = 82.3%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 1.61 (p = 0.215), a **non-significant** difference among levels. *Log odds ratio:* total $I^2$ = 82.3%; omnibus $Q_M$ = 1.61 (p = 0.215), a **non-significant** difference among levels.
![](moderator_analysis_files/figure-html/new_model_panels-4.png){width=960} ::: ### Combined-dataset extensions {#sec-combined-models} Where the construct harmonises cleanly with an original dataset, we re-fit the moderator on the **combined** data. This roughly triples the sample and lets us ask whether the (null) new-extraction patterns hold once the legacy effect sizes are folded in. * **Copying mechanism — Hedges' $g$ (new + Davies 2020):** 227 effect sizes. * **Observer virginity — log OR (new + Jones & DuVal 2019):** 172 effect sizes. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](moderator_analysis_files/figure-html/combined_models_orchards-1.png){width=960} ::: ::: In the combined data the conclusions are unchanged. For **copying mechanism** (Hedges' $g$), total heterogeneity is 85.6% and the among-level test is p = 0.958 ($Q_M$ = 0.00) — a **non-significant** difference between generalized and individual copying. For **observer virginity** (log OR), total heterogeneity is 75.1% and the among-level test is p = 0.723 ($Q_M$ = 0.13), again a **non-significant** difference between virgin and non-virgin observers. --- ## Biologically Meaningful Level Differences {#sec-biological} A non-significant $Q_M$ tells us a moderator does not *explain the heterogeneity*, but it does not tell us whether the **direction and magnitude** of the level differences are biologically interesting. Because the point estimates speak to specific hypotheses raised in the main text, we read them here as well — keeping firmly in mind that every contrast CI below overlaps zero, so these are **directional signals, not demonstrated effects**. ### Observer virginity — a signal that runs *against* the original meta-analysis {#sec-bio-virginity} This is the most noteworthy contrast. Jones & DuVal [@jonesMechanismsSocialInfluence2019] reported — and the present manuscript's discussion repeats as support for the "copy when inexperienced" social-learning strategy — that **virgin (sexually naïve) observers copy more strongly**. Our new extraction shows the **opposite ordering**: virgin observers copied *less* than non-virgins (Hedges' $g$ = 0.09 for virgins vs 0.36 for non-virgins; log OR = 0.16 vs 0.66). The difference is not statistically distinct (CIs overlap zero), but the *sign* is reversed relative to the classic result. When the new data are pooled with the original Jones & DuVal binary data, the contrast all but disappears (non-virgin OR = 1.91 vs virgin OR = 1.75). The takeaway is not that the original finding is wrong, but that the **updated, broader evidence base no longer reproduces the virgin-copies-more pattern**, and the new studies, if anything, point the other way. This is precisely the kind of context-dependence the discussion invokes, and it argues for treating the virginity-conditionality claim with more caution than the original synthesis implied. ### Demonstration cue: copulation versus mere association {#sec-bio-cue} Within the dominant *visual* modality, restricting to the two well-populated cue types shows copying trends **stronger when the social cue is a copulation than mere association** (Hedges' $g$ = 0.36 vs 0.11). This is the biologically expected direction — a completed copulation is a less ambiguous, higher-value social signal than spatial proximity alone — and it offers a concrete, testable prediction for future work, even though the contrast is not significant here. ### Copying mechanism: generalized copying is not the weaker form {#sec-bio-mechanism} The introduction motivates *generalized* copying (forming a preference for a phenotype rather than an individual) as the route by which mate-choice copying could drive the cultural transmission of preferences and shape sexual selection. Reassuringly for that argument, generalized copying is **no weaker than individual copying** in the combined data (Hedges' $g$ = 0.43 generalized vs 0.44 individual) — the evolutionarily consequential form of the behaviour is at least as strong as the individual-recognition form. ### Social-cue modality: a sampling gap, not a result {#sec-bio-modality} The modality comparison (visual $g$ = 0.22, chemical = 0.31, acoustic = 0.08) cannot be read biologically: the chemical and acoustic levels rest on a handful of effect sizes from one to three studies and are confounded with particular species. Its real value is as a **map of where the evidence is missing** — the mate-choice-copying literature is overwhelmingly visual, even though the field's own inclusion criteria explicitly admit chemical and acoustic cues. Broadening sensory sampling is a clear opportunity. --- ## Heterogeneity After Moderators {#sec-heterogeneity-after} The point of this exercise was to see whether any single moderator could absorb a meaningful share of the heterogeneity that the intercept models left unexplained. The table below collects the total $I^2$ and omnibus $Q_M$ for every model fitted in this chapter. ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Interpretation {#sec-interpretation} Across every model, the pattern is the same: **total heterogeneity remains high and no single moderator's omnibus test is significant.** None of copying mechanism, social-cue modality, demonstration sub-type, or observer virginity reduces the heterogeneity to a degree that the among-level test can detect, on either effect-size scale, and the two combined-dataset extensions confirm this once the legacy data are added. The marginalised level means overlap substantially, and partial $I^2$ stays concentrated at the residual effect-size and species levels — precisely the components the overall synthesis flagged as carrying the bulk of the variance. This is a genuinely informative null. It tells us that the heterogeneity is **not** explained by the most obvious experimental and ecological axes available in the current data, and it sharpens the case made in the discussion: the moderators that matter are likely finer-grained (specific paradigms, demonstrator quality, ecological context) or interactive, and the dataset is not yet large or balanced enough — particularly for the non-visual modalities and for any moderator absent from the legacy datasets — to resolve them. We therefore present these as **hypothesis-generating** results and echo the discussion's recommendation that future, ideally pre-registered, syntheses test these moderators explicitly as the evidence base grows. That said, the null heterogeneity result and the [directional level differences](#sec-biological) are two different readings of the same models, and the second is where the biological interest lies. The standout is **observer virginity**: the updated data do not reproduce — and trend against — the virgin-copies-more pattern of the original synthesis, a nuance worth carrying into the discussion even though the contrast is not statistically distinct. ::: {.callout-note} ## Caveats These models are exploratory and were not pre-registered. Several levels (Chemical and Acoustic modality in particular) rest on very few studies and are confounded with individual species, so their estimates are unstable. Absence of a significant $Q_M$ is *not* evidence that a moderator has no effect — only that the current data cannot resolve one. ::: --- ## Session Info ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sessionInfo() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` R version 4.6.0 (2026-04-24) Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin23 Running under: macOS Tahoe 26.5.1 Matrix products: default BLAS: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.6/Resources/lib/libRblas.0.dylib LAPACK: /Library/Frameworks/R.framework/Versions/4.6/Resources/lib/libRlapack.dylib;
